natural: to build up a cool, confident image / to rack up 'aura points'
whoUses: Gen Z / Gen Alpha, nationwide, mostly online (TikTok/Instagram)
usageContext: 'Farmar' is straight from gaming (grinding for resources); 'aura' here means presence/charisma, borrowed from the international 'aura points' meme. Someone 'farms aura' by doing something that reads as effortlessly stylish or confident, on purpose or not.
warningNotes: Very online/current — expect it to age fast, the way most 'aura'-meme vocabulary does.

examples.text: Ele chegou de óculos escuros na sala, só farmando aura.
examples.translation: He walked into the room in sunglasses, just farming aura.

natural: calculated/inauthentic content or persona, engineered to perform well on social media
usageContext: Describes a post, a look, or even a whole personality that feels manufactured purely to please the algorithm rather than genuine — mildly mocking, calling out try-hard content.

examples.text: Esse vídeo é tão algoritmado, dá pra ver que é tudo calculado.
examples.translation: This video is so algorithm-baited, you can tell it's all calculated.

natural: someone acting robotic, predictable, or without a distinct personality of their own
whoUses: Gen Z, nationwide, online and in speech
usageContext: Borrowed directly from English gaming slang and used identically in Portuguese sentences (no translation, just dropped in). Mildly insulting — implies someone is just going through the motions, not a real original opinion.
warningNotes: Can sting if said about a real person to their face — usually used to describe strangers/public figures/situations, not close friends.

examples.text: Todo mundo respondendo igual nos comentários, parece um bando de NPC.
examples.translation: Everyone answering the same in the comments, it's like a bunch of NPCs.

natural: delusional, especially about a crush or a hoped-for outcome
usageContext: Adopted wholesale from English internet slang with Portuguese phonetic spelling. Usually self-deprecating and affectionate rather than a real insult — 'tô delulu' means you know you're hoping for something unlikely.

examples.text: Eu sei que ele não vai me chamar, mas eu tô bem delulu hoje.
examples.translation: I know he's not going to text me, but I'm feeling pretty delulu today.

natural: nailed it / killed it / owned the moment
whoUses: widely known across age groups by now, originally youth/LGBT+ social media slang
usageContext: Praises someone who said or did something so on-point it settled the matter completely — a devastating comeback, a perfect outfit, a mic-drop moment. Older and more established than most terms in this file; well past being a fleeting meme.

examples.text: Ela respondeu na hora e lacrou geral.
examples.translation: She clapped back instantly and absolutely killed it.

natural: freaked out / lost it — can be panic, excitement, or anger depending on context
usageContext: Flexible — 'surtei' can mean anything from genuine anxiety ('surtei com a prova') to over-the-top excitement ('a galera surtou com o show'). Context and tone decide which.

examples.text: A plateia surtou quando a banda entrou no palco.
examples.translation: The crowd lost it when the band walked onstage.

whoUses: everyone informally, especially younger speakers, nationwide
usageContext: Slotted directly in front of an adjective the way 'muito' would be: 'mó legal' ('really cool'), 'mó chato' ('so annoying'). Purely spoken/informal-written register — never in formal contexts.

literal: cookie-maker / to give a cookie (dog-training reference: a treat for good behavior)
natural: someone who fishes for compliments or attention online, often with fake-humble posts
whoUses: originated in LGBT+ online communities, now mainstream Gen Z/millennial slang nationwide
usageContext: 'Biscoiteiro' describes the attention-seeker; 'dar biscoito' is what commenters do when they take the bait and shower praise. Classic example: posting a shirtless gym photo captioned 'ugh, need to lose weight' to farm reassurance.

examples.text: Ela postou de propósito pra receber biscoito nos comentários.
examples.translation: She posted on purpose to fish for compliments in the comments.

literal: 'launched the fierce one' — deliberately misspelled as 'lansei/lanso a braba' in its meme form
usageContext: A meme phrase with no literal logic behind it — used purely as a stock reaction when someone does something impressive, savage, or funny. The deliberate misspelling ('lansei') is part of the joke and often kept even in writing.
warningNotes: A meme phrase, not standard grammar — reproducing the 'wrong' spelling is intentional, not an error to correct.

examples.text: Ela deu um fora nele na frente de todo mundo — lançou a braba!
examples.translation: She dumped him in front of everyone — she went off!

literal: from English 'to ship' (a relationship), verbified with the Portuguese -ar ending
natural: to root for two people (real or fictional) to be a couple
whoUses: fandom communities and Gen Z generally, nationwide
usageContext: A textbook example of how English loanwords get grammatically absorbed into Portuguese — 'ship' becomes a regular -ar verb: eu shippo, ela shippa, nós shippamos. Used constantly in fandom/celebrity-couple contexts.

examples.text: Eu shippo demais esses dois, eles ficariam perfeitos juntos.
examples.translation: I ship these two so hard, they'd be perfect together.

natural: a romantic interest / someone you like
whoUses: everyone under ~40, nationwide — fully absorbed into everyday speech, not just internet slang anymore
usageContext: Used exactly like 'paquera' used to be — 'meu crush' (my crush), 'ele é meu crush' (he's my crush). So embedded that it barely reads as a loanword to younger speakers anymore.

examples.text: Meu crush curtiu minha foto, socorro.
examples.translation: My crush liked my photo, help.

literal: deliberately mispronounced 'calabresa' (a type of sausage), swapping the ending -a for -o
natural: cringe / embarrassing / over-the-top in a funny way
whoUses: nationwide, went viral via a reality-TV (BBB) argument and a comedian's earlier bit
usageContext: Part of a small family of joke-words (calabreso, mussarelo, lumbrigo) that mangle ordinary nouns for comic effect, used to gently mock someone acting embarrassingly or theatrically — lighthearted, not genuinely cruel.
warningNotes: Tied to a specific viral moment (a 2024 reality-show argument) — worth knowing what it references, but it may already read as dated/last-year to some speakers.

examples.text: Ele discutindo daquele jeito na frente de todo mundo foi muito calabreso.
examples.translation: Him arguing like that in front of everyone was so cringe.

literal: candy wrapper (the shell/wrapper of a hard candy)
whoUses: nationwide, popularized by a 2024 viral song rooted in vaquejada (rodeo) culture
usageContext: The image is a candy stuck to its wrapper in your pocket — impossible to separate. Warm and affectionate, used for a best friend you're always glued to.

examples.text: A gente é casca de bala desde a infância.
examples.translation: We've been inseparable best friends since childhood.

literal: from English 'tank' (the gaming role that absorbs damage), verbified
natural: to endure/tough out something difficult; intankável = unbearable
whoUses: originally gamer slang, now mainstream Gen Z/millennial usage nationwide
usageContext: 'Preciso tankar essa reunião' (I have to tank/endure this meeting) extends the gaming sense (absorbing hits so others don't have to) to everyday hardship. 'Intankável' describes something so bad even that isn't possible — often used ironically about current events ('tankar o Brasil').

examples.text: Vou ter que tankar esse plantão até de manhã.
examples.translation: I'm going to have to tough out this shift until morning.
